---
title: "Evidence without the victory lap"
subtitle: "One sharp teaching failure, one useful null, one promising prototype"
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
---

::: {.page-lead}
The claim under test is narrow: **does an attribution ranking recover variables
with large known total effects, rather than variables that merely sit close to
the outcome?** Because every dataset is simulated from a known structural model,
the answer can be scored against frozen intervention truth.
:::

## The teaching DAG: make the mismatch impossible to miss

The five-node toy combines a chain, a fork, and a collider. `ClinicVisit` is a
descendant of the outcome and has zero total effect on it, but is deliberately
highly predictive.

| Variable | Known total-effect share | Ordinary SHAP | Structural Causal SHAP |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Diet | 27.6% | 8.8% | 26.0% |
| Climate | 37.9% | 15.4% | 43.7% |
| Hydration | 34.5% | 30.2% | 13.8% |
| **ClinicVisit** | **0.0%** | **45.6%** | **16.6%** |

Ordinary SHAP produces a negative rank correlation with truth
($\tau\approx-0.33$); the structural version flips it positive
($\tau\approx+0.33$). The residual 16.6% on `ClinicVisit` is also instructive:
structural Shapley contribution is not identical to a global total-effect
ranking, especially under symmetric coalition averaging. The demonstration is
a stress test, not a claim of perfect recovery.

::: {.source-note}
Source: committed
[`attribution.csv`](https://github.com/andystats/causal-shap-target-dags/blob/main/app/bundles/toy_chain_fork_collider/stages/attribution.csv)
and
[`true_effects.json`](https://github.com/andystats/causal-shap-target-dags/blob/main/app/bundles/toy_chain_fork_collider/true_effects.json).
:::

## The NASA-topology simulation: ordering alone is a null

The larger experiment uses the public NASA SA-07566 renal-stone DAG topology
with synthetic coefficients and outcomes. The topology is source-aligned; the
numbers below are **not NASA effect estimates**.

| Method | Kendall $\tau$ vs truth | Top-5 recovery | Proximity-bias index | Mass within 2 hops |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Exact TreeSHAP | 0.522 | 0.60 | 1.082 | 83.1% |
| Matched ordinary interventional SHAP | 0.506 | 0.60 | 1.051 | 81.6% |
| **DAG-asymmetric SHAP** | **0.528** | **0.60** | **1.051** | **81.2%** |
| **Structural prototype** | **0.794** | **1.00** | **−0.113** | **38.1%** |
| Frozen intervention truth | 1.000 | 1.00 | — | 42.1% |

Paired bootstrap intervals include zero for the differences between matched
ordinary and DAG-asymmetric SHAP across rank recovery and proximity measures.
That is the project’s most useful negative result:

::: {.pullquote}
Respecting a causal order does not, by itself, make the coalition value causal.
The descendants must be allowed to respond to the intervention.
:::

![Attribution mass by directed distance to the outcome on the NASA-topology simulation.](assets/figures/nasa_distortion.png){fig-alt="Comparison of attribution mass by distance to the outcome for ordinary, DAG-ordered, structural, and truth rankings"}

## What changed in the structural prototype

The ordinary and ordering-only methods evaluate the predictor against different
feature backgrounds or coalition orders. The structural prototype instead uses

$$v_{do}(S)=\mathbb E[f(X)\mid do(X_S=x_S)],$$

regenerating unfixed descendants through estimated structural equations. That
is the experimental contrast that moves mass away from the outcome neighborhood
and toward variables with large known total effects.

The prototype currently uses 32 evaluation records, 32 permutations, and 32
Monte Carlo draws per coalition. It is therefore evidence of a promising
mechanism—not a stabilized estimator comparison.

## The evidence ladder

<div class="evidence-ladder">
  <div><span>1</span><strong>Mechanism</strong><p>The toy DAG exposes the predictive/causal mismatch.</p></div>
  <div><span>2</span><strong>Negative control</strong><p>Ordering alone is statistically tied with ordinary SHAP.</p></div>
  <div><span>3</span><strong>Prototype</strong><p>Intervention propagation improves known-truth recovery.</p></div>
  <div class="future"><span>4</span><strong>Required next</strong><p>Scale, repeated seeds, paired uncertainty, graph sensitivity, action costs.</p></div>
</div>

## Interpretation guardrails

- All datasets and outcomes are synthetic.
- The NASA graph supplies topology only; coefficients are simulation choices.
- A high total-effect rank does not imply that a variable is manipulable.
- A manipulable variable is not automatically ethical, affordable, or robust.
- A causal graph can be wrong; recommendation candidates must be sensitivity-tested
  over plausible structures and mechanisms.

For the full artifact record, see
[`docs/RESULTS.md`](https://github.com/andystats/causal-shap-target-dags/blob/main/docs/RESULTS.md),
[`docs/LIMITATIONS.md`](https://github.com/andystats/causal-shap-target-dags/blob/main/docs/LIMITATIONS.md),
and [Reproduce](reproducibility.qmd).
